quick_facts.label: Indian regulatory context
quick_facts.value: In India, public-issue and securities-market merchant-banking activities are conducted by SEBI-registered merchant bankers; the SEBI regulations address the registered entity, while “investment banker” is the broader job-role title used by employers and professional bodies.

quick_facts.label: Core mandate
quick_facts.value: Investment bankers advise institutional clients on capital raising and strategic transactions, including IPOs and other securities offerings, private placements, mergers, acquisitions, divestitures, restructuring, and debt or equity advisory.

quick_facts.label: Relevant education routes
quick_facts.value: Common entry backgrounds include undergraduate or postgraduate study in business administration, finance, commerce, economics, statistics, or another analytical discipline; an MBA is a common, but not universally mandatory, route.

quick_facts.label: CFA Program relevance
quick_facts.value: The CFA Program is a three-level professional credential. CFA Institute identifies investment banking as a potential transaction-focused career path, but the credential is not presented as a statutory licence to practise merchant banking in India.

quick_facts.label: India-specific learning option
quick_facts.value: NISM and the Association of Investment Bankers of India offer a Certificate Program in Merchant Banking covering SEBI-regulated transactions, corporate finance, modelling, valuation, ethics, and an internship; it is positioned for aspiring professionals and finance-domain learners.

sections.heading: What an Investment Banker Does
paragraphs: Investment bankers help corporate, institutional, and sometimes government clients raise capital and execute strategic financial transactions. Work can include IPO and other issue preparation, book building, prospectus-related work, private placements, debt and equity advisory, mergers and acquisitions, divestitures, restructuring, and client presentations.

In the Indian public-issue context, SEBI rules place particular obligations on lead merchant bankers around issue management. These include clearly allocating responsibilities, supporting disclosures, and carrying out due diligence related to offer documents; the precise allocation depends on the transaction and participating intermediaries.
bullets: Analyse companies, sectors, capital structures, financial statements, and transaction alternatives.
Build financial models and valuations; prepare pitch books, information materials, and client presentations.
Coordinate with issuers, investors, legal advisers, auditors, registrars, stock exchanges, and other transaction participants.
For regulated capital-markets transactions, work within SEBI disclosure, due-diligence, and intermediary requirements.

sections.heading: Entry Routes in India
paragraphs: A practical entry route is a bachelor’s degree followed by analyst-level recruitment, internships, or relevant experience in financial analysis, advisory, trading, consulting, corporate finance, or capital markets. Employers commonly value business, finance, commerce, economics, statistics, and other analytical academic backgrounds, alongside demonstrated quantitative ability and communication skills.

An MBA or PGDM with finance coursework can support a transition into investment banking, especially when paired with internships and transaction-relevant skills. NISM’s merchant-banking certificate programme is an India-specific supplementary route: it is aimed at aspiring professionals and finance learners, includes an internship component, and lists MBA/PGDM finance students and candidates with certain professional or legal-study backgrounds among its intended participants.

The CFA Program can complement this route by developing investment analysis, valuation, and finance knowledge across three examination levels. It should be viewed as a professional credential that can strengthen candidacy, rather than as a substitute for employer hiring requirements or SEBI registration of a merchant-banking entity.
bullets: Build evidence of capability through internships, live projects, financial-modelling work, equity or credit research, transaction support, or corporate-finance roles.
Target entry titles such as Analyst or Junior Banker; employers’ titles and selection processes differ.
Use MBA, CFA, NISM, CA/CS/CMA-related, or legal/regulatory learning as complementary pathways where they fit the candidate’s prior education and target team.

sections.heading: Skills to Build
paragraphs: The role combines technical corporate-finance work with client service. Core technical capability includes financial-statement analysis, forecasting, financial modelling, valuation, capital-structure analysis, and understanding of securities markets and transactions. Clear writing and persuasive presentation are equally important because bankers use pitch books and other materials to explain recommendations and win mandates.

For India-facing capital-markets work, regulatory literacy, careful documentation, professional ethics, and due diligence matter. NISM’s merchant-banking curriculum specifically includes capital-markets regulation and compliance, IPO and corporate-action due diligence, financial modelling and valuation, and professional writing and interpersonal skills.
bullets: Financial modelling, valuation, forecasting, and analytical problem-solving.
Financial-statement, company, industry, and market analysis.
Excel-based analysis and effective presentation of conclusions to non-specialists.
Written communication, public speaking, persuasion, relationship building, and teamwork.
Regulatory awareness, ethics, attention to detail, recordkeeping, and ability to work under transaction deadlines.

sections.heading: Where the Role Is Performed
paragraphs: Investment bankers may work in investment banks, the investment-banking or corporate-finance divisions of financial institutions, and firms that provide securities-market advisory services. In India, SEBI’s description of permissible merchant-banking activity includes public-issue management and underwriting connected to it, international debt or equity offerings, private placements, government-securities dealership, and corporate advisory related to takeovers, acquisitions, and disinvestment.

The work is client-facing and transaction-driven, involving coordination among issuers and multiple professional intermediaries. The pace can be demanding; CFA Institute describes day-to-day investment-banking work as fast-paced and potentially stressful, and notes that it may not align with a standard 40-hour workweek.
bullets: Corporate-finance, capital-markets, mergers-and-acquisitions, debt-capital-markets, and equity-capital-markets teams.
Transaction teams working with issuers, investors, legal counsel, auditors, and SEBI-registered market intermediaries.

sections.heading: Typical Career Progression
paragraphs: A common progression begins with Analyst-level work focused on analysis, modelling, research, materials preparation, and transaction execution support. Associate roles generally require more experience and broaden responsibility for workstreams and junior-team coordination. With substantial experience, progression may continue through Vice President, Senior Vice President, Director, and Managing Director or equivalent senior-banker titles.

Actual timelines, titles, promotion standards, and compensation structures vary widely by firm, product group, city, market conditions, individual performance, and transaction flow. Internships and relevant early experience can be valuable for access and networking, but do not guarantee conversion or promotion.
bullets: Analyst / Junior Banker: analytical and execution-support responsibilities.
Associate: greater ownership of transaction workstreams and team coordination.
Vice President, Director, Managing Director or equivalent: senior execution, client coverage, origination, and leadership responsibilities.
